speaker: interviewer
text: --95. And Mr. Eizenstein, you were telling us how you had heard that your town was liberated and you chose to leave Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the authorities. They deny me. They say they need me there. And I thought to myself, I want to go. Who believed what they did-- the Germans? They show us-- every night I went to see a movie. They show us in the movies what the Germans doing, and what they-- they did, and what they doing. We didn't believe them. Everybody, no matter 99 and three quarter percent from the Russian nation-- they used to say that's propaganda. No one believed-- no one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they deny me to-- to go-- to volunteer to the army or to go home. So what I did-- what I did, every penny I got-- my watch, everything-- I went to the bazaar. I sold it. And instead to take weapons, I took bread. I bought bread. There, you don't need weapons. So I brought up bread in the black market. Right then, we used to get so much bread. We used to get lots. But I mean, I couldn't take it when I'm not working, you know? I want to disappear. So I went-- nobody to know what's going on. And there was a girl what I knew her very well. She gave me a pass. And I filled it up myself. I beg her for a pass. She didn't-- who was thinking I'm going run away, you know? And this girl worked in a--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the office.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in an official office?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She gave me the application for a pass. While in Russia, you can't move. You can't go no place without the pass. So gave  me the pass. I filled up the pass, you know, for a week. And I went to a bazaar. I bought bread. And I filled up myself with bread. And I jump in, in a coal train, from coal, in the train. I couldn't go in a passenger train. I need-- you know, I need a what do you call them? A ticket. I couldn't get a ticket. Searching I don't know where, until I got into a Chkalov. In Chkalov, they stop the train. They start to look in the train, the army. So I jumped up, and I went into them. I says, I want to volunteer. Here's mine pass. Here's mine-- two weeks, or three weeks, or a week, I made myself a [INAUDIBLE] on the pass. I want to volunteer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which army and which unit was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Russian army--

speaker: interviewer
text: The same Russian army.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Russia. This is Russia, Chkalov, yeah-- a very big city. They stop the train from the coal. You know, and the-- right away, the army start to look, you know, who knows for what? You know, they know what they're looking for-- runaways, you know, deserters. Any why should I be a deserter or runaway? Right away, I come into the guys and I says-- anyways, they believe me. And they took me into the-- to the station, train station. And they give me away to the command from the police force. You know why? I was a civilian, know-- to the police force. And they told them, you know, I got-- I got all the papers. They can't arrest me. I'm not a criminal. And I tell them the truth. And they say to me, OK, Borys Gregoravitch, we got-- we don't know what to do with you. But if you want to volunteer to the army, we get you to the army. I says, I'm hungry. Anyways, they gave me food. And after a few hours, they call me to get out. And there was a little army truck. And they took me to a camp. And they put me in, in a barrack, 71 barrack. I never forget the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told me, you can go to eat. You can go to the city. I didn't have money or nothing, so I didn't go into the city-- but food in restaurant, drinks, anything you want. And I am alone one day, two days, three days. No one come ask no questions. After a week, some guys come in with big noses. They're Jewish. So I says, to them, are you [NON-ENGLISH]. He says yes, what are you doing here? I start to talk. They don't know himself. They are resting. And they took off on the job. They want to go to Poland. And they cut him. And they are from Poland. They hardly speak Russian, just Polish. They're Jewish. And we start to talk what's going to happen. We scared to death. And the next day, next day, start to come in gentiles-- Polish, [? Polaks. ?] So finally, after three weeks, we find out they organizing a-- Stalin give orders organize a Polish army from Polish citizen. And I didn't know that. They told me right away. After that, they told me-- people were they born in Ukraine, they call them [NON-ENGLISH]. That's Polish-Ukrainian. The Russians didn't accept even to their army. They didn't accept [NON-ENGLISH]. No matter what war, they didn't. So they sent all these guys from the-- Stalin give orders all from [NON-ENGLISH] to the Polish army. And what year-- do you remember what year and month that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the beginning from '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was winter, '44, January of something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Winter of 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So then, I was released. You know, I figure out, you know, that's it. Now I'm saved. Anyway, in short and to say, they sent us to [? Suma. ?] There was a point from the Polish organization, from Polish army, that's after [? Kharkov, ?] that's Ukraine. And in [? Suma ?] then was already Polish officers-- uniforms. They give us right away uniform. You know, each one got what they have to get. And this was the base. Then they brought in from Poland-- for Poland, they brought in us Jeeps, and tanks, and planes. We have to put them in boxes. And the food was unbelieve from America.

speaker: interviewer
text: The food was American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was American-- the Jeeps. You know, what they call that vehicles. We got in all American. But the engineers were from America. They put them together. Some of them knew Polish, some of them English. So anyways, we start-- we knew already. And everything was American. I never forget, I had three pounds big of stewing-- beef stew, [INAUDIBLE] and done. Next day, I ate three pints of jam-- never forget-- grape jam. You never-- who saw jam? Anyways, we were there about two months. Every morning training, nothing, exercise-- more or less just exercise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the Jewish soldiers get along with the ones who were not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know about Jewish soldiers at all. I didn't see Jewish soldiers there at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the ones that came in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see-- you didn't see them again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody, nothing. The only thing, every morning, exercise.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after three, four months, they come up in General [INAUDIBLE]. And he says, [? Minjinsky-- ?] it was [NON-ENGLISH], like here-- I don't know, a general I think, or something like that-- nice, chubby guy. And he says, I'm going work with him. He says, we don't have chauffeurs. And he's the [NON-ENGLISH]. He's the boss from the [NON-ENGLISH], from artillery, intelligence artillery. You belong to me. You want it? I says, what you mean by that? Why not a job like that? He says, that's going to be the second front. It's worse like the front. But we go in, in the second front. It's more danger. Why? The airplanes, the artillery, and they know the second front is worse, like the first front. I didn't have choice. And I liked the guy. So right away, I got in, in-- in the barracks specially, from these big shots-- beautiful restaurant, ice cream, you name it, it was there, served like a king. Uniform-- you saw my uniform. And we got our training. We got our vehicles after the America. And then they sent us away to Poland via train, to Lutsk. And then they put us in the bushes. And in the bushes, we organized in the artillery, the [NON-ENGLISH], what they trust to Poland. But they give it to us three.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the train, me and another two guys-- one was Grossman, a lieutenant, is Jewish. His father on-- is from Prague, Czechoslovakia-- owns the factory from Messerschmitt. OK, I'll tell you about him in a second. I-- I ask him, how you survive? We-- I come, you come to Polish army? When Germany invade Czechoslovakia, his father gave him airplane to run away. His father didn't want to run away. I'm telling you the way honest, you know, the way he told me. Very nice looking guy, very intelligent-- and he to over the plane. And he fall down in Poland, besides Ternopil, in [? Bitze, ?] in-- behind enemy line, in Germany. It was then German occupied.

speaker: interviewee
text: He line up there, in a village, like close to a village. And he fall down with the plane there. He got wounded. The farmers picked him up. So one farmer-- anyways, he got married to one farm girl and they got two girls, this Grossman. So he was the only Jewish guy, Grossman. He'd never changed [INAUDIBLE]. So we met there. And we cooperate. We work together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and another guy, they taught us the secrets how to destroy, God forbid, if a Katyusha get hit, or get-- or get captured, the button how to destroy it. You know, it was a push-button, suicide push-button-- I to destroy the Katyusha. So they taught us how. Plus, they train us search in artillery all these cannons-- secret cannons. And my job was to go out with the searching group to places where to put the artillery-- you know, what positions to put them out. That was the most dangerous. That was suicide missions. Or we have to go behind the lines-- that's me, you know with a group-- two or three people they give me to go in, where to put, or where to hit the Germans. You understand me? This was my mission. So we trained. We got our tools. We got our weapons. We got everything. And living was like a king. Every Friday, they brought us nurses. You understand, nurses? No, I-- please explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nurses, from the field nurses. Nurses--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, nurses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They have parties. You understand? They brought us booze. They-- we live like kings, but in the bush. And we didn't find-- then they start to send us-- we say we're going to invade to Poland, Warsaw. Up to that was liberated to Wisla-- Warsaw So we come. The headquarters-- they put it up in Prague, Polish Prague. And we went to [? Otvosk. ?] That was the Polish aristocrats cottages there. So they put us all there, all our tools, the weapons, the whole army. But the [NON-ENGLISH], the general, was in Prague, Polish Prague, in this side Wisla. And they told us they're going to invade Warsaw, Poland. And from then, we're up to Berlin. And they told us, stand and give orders each republic. You understand me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakian, Romanian, Hungarian, Kazakhs, Bulgars-- all these republics go in with their flags and go and invade Poland, Warsaw-- you know, the Wisla pass-- and go to Berlin. All the republics take part. Finally, we prepared. Everything was OK. Here, this was summer in July, June. We come in there, everything ready to go. Stop. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalin got orders from the Americans, from the British-- here goes the secrets. The Americans, British, French told Stalin, you stop here. We go and liberate Warsaw. We go and liberate Poland. We go and liberate Czechoslovakia. You're not moving. If you make a move, we're not going to supply you nothing. You understand me? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: How I know this secret? We're not moving. And every morning come in Poles partisans-- wounded, crippled, sick. Then come in Jewish from the ghetto, from Warsaw ghetto uprising, to us. You know, they didn't go to the general [? staff. ?] They didn't go to generals. We were there, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how they found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on the open. We were in the open, you know, in the-- we were open. We went all over, free. They stopped us. They told us for six months, we can't move. And you know, we put the weapons away-- no weapons, nothing. We went like civilians. Food like-- yeah, you know. Food-- you name it, the biggest, the nicest food money can buy-- supply us. Beautiful restaurants in tents. Even ice cream they supplied us. I don't know where they got it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the partisans used to come in. And we used to come in to the leaders. We couldn't go into the generals. We weren't with us. Our [? surgeon ?] major, you know, the tenants and that-- what's going on? You know, why we don't move? So then they told us the secret. I went to my general. [? Minjinsky ?] was then-- in Polish, they called him-- [POLISH]-- no, what do call him-- [POLISH]. He knew I'm Jewish. I did know he's Jewish. And he told me the whole secret-- not in Jewish. He didn't want to expose himself. And he called me, and says, my son, he says, I'll tell you a secret. And you keep it, you, the secret. He says, Stalin give-- they give to Stalin orders. They're going to liberate Poland. Not enough what they did, the bandits. The Americans, the British-- I call them bandits, killers, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] traitors. They went and they put up fence-- not fence. What do you call them? Plywood. They put up plywood what the ghetto uprising smell was-- you heard the smell from human-- from human flesh. So they went and they put a fence. What do you call them-- from plywood, two story plywood, about eight, 10 foot high, the whole three quarter of Warsaw. You understand? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: As not to see the scream and the flame. We could liberate them, like I could have now a cigarette. We were there. Why they kept us for six months?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the fence went up, did you have any personal dealings with the people from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have personal feelings. They used to come to us, the partisans, for weapons--

speaker: interviewer
text: The dealers, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They want to buy weapons. We didn't sell it to them. We give it to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did deal with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stole it-- we all used to steal weapons for our own self and give it to them. But how much could we get? We couldn't get enough. But some of them were Jewish guys. I spoke to them, in Jewish. You know, they were so miserable. They were so dirty. They were so-- not dirty in the head, but in-- in-- you know, dirty in the faces, you know, with their clothes-- messed up. And the smell-- and they say, why they don't help us? Like I told you, I went to mine [POLISH] and I ask him. And he told me the secret. I couldn't pass the secret to nobody. But the people knew-- the soldiers. Why they kept us for six months when we could liberate all Poland, all the concentration camps? We could liberate them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, in January, you know, they went in the 4th or the 7th. They invade-- they invade-- Warsaw. But everything, when we went in there, it was nothing there. We went 70 kilometers. We didn't see a German. Everything was paid off. Why the Polish army, the Polish government, ran away to Bri-- to Britain? You understand? They wanted liberate Poland. They went to send the Polish. British army-- they sent-- they sent it to Casino, Monte Casino, in Italy. They-- they kill them all there. They blow up-- with the kos-- the what do you call them? I'm so, you know, upset-- injustice. And now the-- now the bodies-- the British, the American, they liberate the-- but they are killers. We could-- OK, why they didn't bomb places? Why they didn't send-- even Jewish brigade they send to Yugoslavia. Why didn't send to liberate the concentration camps? Why they didn't send-- they sent paratroops all over. Why they didn't send to liberate the-- the concentration camp? It was so easy. I was talking to people from concentration camps, the survivors, where there were three kilometers American-- three kilometers before the concentration camps. You understand? And they didn't liberate them. They wait three, four days to liberate them. And the Germans took care of that. Why? They was-- everything was made up. You know what I mean? And when we liberated-- when we went in 70 kilometers, I liberate-- not me, I mean. I started to say I-- our unit, we liberated the first camp. I don't know the name. We-- they wore brown uniforms, yellow with paint, you know, like a sign like that. And it was just girls-- cut up the hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we caught the whole thing. We caught the leaders. We caught the Gestapo. We caught everything. It was a little-- I think a [? manufactory ?] there-- something they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a work camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't have time to get in. I didn't investigate. I was just a soldier. But the only thing, these peop-- these soldiers start to throw food. And they start to scream-- who knows German, or Jewish, or Polish-- you know, not to let the people get food. And I went in with the-- with my [POLISH], [? Minjinsky, ?] with the command-- with the command, and while we were on command. And-- and I grabbed the speaker, or they give it to me. I don't remember. I was wild. And I told in Jewish not to eat-- not to eat. They're going-- they going take care of them, while they're coming in-- the Red Cross. The-- the Russian army come in a special unit-- you know, doctors. And they're going to care of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went in. [SIGHS] We didn't have a-- a time to stay to talk to them, you know? I was a soldier. We went in ahead. We liberate lots. And after a few days, we went out-- you know-- you know, we went ahead. And I got another-- I got lots to say. But I know you got no-- not enough film and time. But I never forget-- You can continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: --one story you should know. We passing with the-- I was going, with the command. I was going with my command in the front. In the back, the tanks and artillery. And here, I saw on the right side a Jewish man with a beard, with a talis, and maybe a few dozen woman or kids-- I don't know-- maybe men, but a group with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, please--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember nothing, like I says. In my position, what I was, when I saw, you know, I can't tell you. And I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the only thing-- the minute I passed them, we heard shots. So I stopped. And down I look. The guy is laying with the beard-- the Jewish rabbi, laying with the beard and the talis, blood all over him. And two Russians standing--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry-- two Russians were standing. You say [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. And a Jew doesn't have money. How it can be? You know, and they Communist. And they say-- [INAUDIBLE] you know? Anyways, right away we grabbed in army police. And the [POLISH] I did know then is Jewish. He stopped them. And he says, [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- to kill them. And the kids fall in, and the Jewish guy, in the rabbi-- he was a rabbi, I don't know-- a long beard. I don't know from where he comes-- from the bush. It was-- it was filled, the bush. Anyways, I wanted-- so they-- not-- in Russia the [NON-ENGLISH] men, to kill them, is they have to take off the clothes-- army clothes. They took off one guy the clothes, from the right side-- nice tall guy. And they took his clothes, and they shot him. And the-- the other guy, that went [INAUDIBLE] his clothes, was in his-- in his back was Lenin and Stalin, beautiful tattoos. The whole body, his back-- I never forget the picture still-- Lenin and Stalin, so beautiful made.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the commander says-- from the right away come in, from the army police-- he says, according to law, we can't shoot a guy like that. It's Lenin Stalin. So they put him back kind of clothes. And they covered that, you know, the hand and his legs. And they threw him in a Jeep. And they took him away. I don't know what they did to him. But it's-- it's lots-- lots to say done. What can I tell you? That's-- and then we went to Berlin. We didn't went in one day. You worked your way to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We work our way up, up to-- to Berlin. But before Berlin, we got stuck. We didn't have-- we went so fast. We didn't have enough gas. And I got experience from the partisans. So was lots of tanks, vehicles overturned, burned, damaged. And I went with the guys. And I says, come on, guys. I show-- we get the-- we get gas. And I was having with mine very secret papers to bring in to the command, to the Russian command some secret-- very secret paper. Plus, we got a cannon to mine-- hitched up a cannon. I don't know, this was the most secret cannon, a small one. But this is was so powerful-- very secret. It was covered. And we were going, you know, with-- with the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was lots of officers-- mine [POLISH] was, and the Russian officers. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: A few more minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went in, in the field. We got in with the pipes and sucked out gas-- benzene-- who knows what it was? From tanks, from all over-- through into mine tank, and we made it to the bunker. I mean, we left the cannon to the command. And then we took us for a trip to show the Hitler's bunker. We got pictures. But I don't know where the pictures are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember to describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I describe it? Was in the left side was the bunker. Everything was blowing up. And I even-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? We have time to finish the description.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? You know, and when the guys, even-- and that's what hurts, too. Even the flag, when they put it up there, was a Polish too, or one Polish soldier. But they don't say it. They say this is Russian soldiers from our unit, you know, what they fighting to go there. But the most important thing is why England, America, and France sold out Poland. It's not my country, but a citizen from Poland. And Poland gave me a name. And Poland gave me a uniform. And Poland gave me the right to fight. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why they did that to Poland? They sent away Sikorski. They blow him up. The leader from Sikorski-- they sent his army to Monte Casino to get killed-- the British Polish. You understand me? And us, they didn't let to liberate Poland. Why can they do things like that? And the world doesn't say nothing-- not the Poles. Everybody scared. It's conspiracy. I don't know why. I never, never forgive them.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape then.
